reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Clear guardrail-vs-alarm distinction is called out early and reinforced in the Info callout — important for users who might confuse the two tools.
Each guardrail type gets its own H2 with a focused 'When to set this' paragraph and a per-property table. The structure is consistent and scannable.
Strong 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' pairing with concrete scenarios, including an honest 'solo developer' caveat.
FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-mechanics, and comparison questions well — the 'resource count vs CloudFormation limits' and 'Lambda cost per MB' entries are genuinely useful.
The Warning callout about container-resource-limit applying to the task (not per container) in multi-container workloads surfaces a real gotcha.
Good cross-links to related guardrail pages (/guardrails/deployment, /guardrails/security-and-data-protection, /guardrails/databases) without over-explaining those pages.
problems: Critical gap: the page never shows how to configure guardrails. The source file `types/stacktape-config/guardrails.d.ts` lives in `types/stacktape-config/`, the same directory as all other config-file types, strongly implying guardrails are configured in stacktape.ts. Yet the page only says 'Configure them in the Stacktape Console' with no code example and no Console workflow. A first-time developer is left with no actionable path.

Ungrounded defaults: the page states the function memory default is 1024 MB and the timeout default is 20 seconds in the context of guardrail evaluation (e.g. 'a guardrail with maxMemoryMB: 512 would block functions that rely on the default'). These values do not appear in guardrails.d.ts. If they are wrong, readers will configure guardrails that silently pass or block the wrong functions.

Fabricated error message format: the 'Guardrail violations' section shows specific error strings ('Function "processImages" has memory 4096MB which exceeds the limit of 2048MB.') that are not present in the source. These may be inaccurate and create false expectations.

The 'How resource limit guardrails work' section claims guardrails run during `preview-changes` and `delete` but the source does not confirm this claim. The source only defines the guardrail types, not the commands that trigger evaluation.

The FAQ answer for 'Do guardrails run during stacktape delete?' states that resource-scoped guardrails only run during delete 'if a project configuration file is available' — this conditional behavior is not present in the source and reads as speculation.
mandatoryFixes: Show how to configure guardrails with a concrete example. If guardrails are defined in the stacktape.ts config file (as strongly implied by the `types/stacktape-config/guardrails.d.ts` location), add a `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with TypeScript using `defineConfig` that shows at least one guardrail definition. If guardrails are genuinely Console-only (org-level server settings), add a brief Console-workflow description and explicitly state that no config file entry is needed — the current page says neither, leaving the first-time reader with nowhere to go.

Remove or re-source the claimed defaults (1024 MB memory, 20-second timeout) in the Function Memory Limit and Function Timeout Limit sections. These values do not appear in `guardrails.d.ts`. Either link to the Lambda function page where those defaults are stated, or drop the numbers and write 'functions without an explicit memory value are evaluated against Stacktape's Lambda default — see [Lambda function](/resources/compute/lambda-function) for the default value'.

Remove the specific error message format strings from the 'Guardrail violations' section. They are not sourced in `guardrails.d.ts` or any provided source file. Replace with a description of the error type and category (e.g. 'Stacktape throws a GUARDRAIL error naming the offending resource and the limit it exceeded') without quoting a fabricated string.
optionalImprovements: Add a quick-reference overview table near the top listing all five resource-limit guardrail types (function-memory-limit, function-timeout-limit, container-resource-limit, resource-type-restriction, resource-count-limit) with one-line descriptions. This lets returning developers jump directly to the guardrail they need rather than reading from the top.

The 'Combining guardrails' section describes a common production setup in prose but would be more scannable as a table: guardrail type | recommended value | rationale. Tables are cited more often by AI engines and are easier to skim.

Clarify whether each guardrail property is optional (e.g. can you set maxCpu without maxMemoryMB in container-resource-limit?). The source types use `?:` for all properties, suggesting both can be omitted independently — explicitly stating this removes a common 'do I have to set both?' question.

The FAQ entry 'Can I set different resource limits for different stages?' recommends creating separate organizations. For most teams this is operationally expensive. Add a sentence noting this is the only current mechanism, so the reader knows it's a real constraint, not an oversight.

Consider adding a one-line callout or note that `resource-type-restriction` accepts Stacktape's internal type identifier strings (e.g. 'web-service', 'open-search-domain') and point to where the full list of valid type identifiers can be found — new users won't know these strings off the top of their head.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Covers all five scoped guardrail types (function-memory-limit, function-timeout-limit, container-resource-limit, resource-type-restriction, resource-count-limit) accurately against the source type definitions.
Per-guardrail property tables are clean and scannable — engineers can immediately see what to configure.
The Info callout explicitly distinguishes guardrails (preventive) from alarms (reactive), which is a key conceptual split the section instructions require and many similar pages miss.
When-to-use and When-NOT-to-use are peer H2 sections with concrete scenarios — not marketing-tier vague bullets.
FAQ breadth is good: Stacktape-specific, AWS cost mechanics, and comparison questions are all represented across the 9 entries.
The 'Combining guardrails' section gives a realistic production combination that a real team would copy directly.
problems: Default value claims are ungrounded in the provided source files. The page states 'Stacktape uses the default of 1024 MB' and 'default of 20 seconds' when no explicit `memory` or `timeout` is set on a Lambda. These numbers do not appear in `guardrails.d.ts` or any other provided source. If the Stacktape Lambda default differs from standard AWS defaults, these statements will mislead engineers into thinking a function is guardrail-compliant when it is not.

Invented error message format. The specific error strings shown in 'Guardrail violations' (e.g., 'Function "processImages" has memory 4096MB which exceeds the limit of 2048MB.') do not appear in the provided source. If the actual message format differs, engineers debugging a failed deploy will be confused.

Ungrounded behavioral claim in FAQ: the answer to 'Do guardrails run during `stacktape delete`?' states 'resource-scoped guardrails only run if a project configuration file is available' and 'Stage, region, and command restrictions always apply.' Neither the guardrails.d.ts nor any other provided source file describes this distinction. This is a specific operational claim that could mislead engineers.

No code examples showing a compliant (or non-compliant) Stacktape resource configuration. Engineers arriving after a guardrail violation need to see what 'memory: 512' or 'cpu: 2' looks like in context, not just prose descriptions. The page is about configuring guardrail policies but says nothing concrete about adjusting the resource that violated them.

The `container-resource-limit` section states the guardrail checks `resources.cpu` and `resources.memory` property paths, and that 'If a workload does not set these values, the guardrail does not block it.' Neither the specific property paths nor the no-explicit-config behavior is grounded in the provided source.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or hedge the specific default values (1024 MB memory, 20 seconds timeout) asserted for unset Lambda properties, since these are not in the provided source. Replace with: 'When a function does not explicitly set a `memory` value, consult the Lambda function defaults documented on the [Lambda function](/resources/compute/lambda-function) page — the guardrail compares against that value.' Do not assert a specific number unless the Lambda function type definition confirms it.

Remove the specific error message format examples in 'Guardrail violations' or qualify them as illustrative ('the error message identifies the resource name, the configured value, and the configured limit'). Do not show a specific string format that cannot be verified against the source.

Remove the ungrounded delete-operation behavioral nuance from the FAQ ('resource-scoped guardrails only run if a project configuration file is available' for delete). Either omit the distinction or note that the exact evaluation scope on delete is not documented here and link to the relevant CLI page.

Add at least one code example showing a Stacktape resource configuration that complies with a common guardrail scenario — for instance, a Lambda function with `memory: 512` and `timeout: 15` alongside a note that these values satisfy a guardrail capping memory at 1024 MB. Without this, engineers fixing a violation have no concrete reference for how to adjust their config. Use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` as required.
optionalImprovements: The 'container-resource-limit' warning callout correctly notes that the limit applies to the whole task in a multi-container workload. Consider also noting what happens when a workload has no explicit CPU/memory set — based on source, the guardrail does not fire, but engineers may assume it does. A brief clarifying sentence prevents confusion.

The 'resource-type-restriction' section lists example type strings (open-search-domain, redis-cluster, batch-job) but does not tell engineers where to find the complete list of valid `StpResourceType` string values. A link to the resource type reference or a note that the Console UI lists available values would reduce friction.

The FAQ question 'Can I set different resource limits for different stages?' recommends creating separate organizations as the workaround. This is technically correct per the source (guardrails are org-level), but it is a heavy operational recommendation. A brief qualification — 'if you need per-stage limits, separate organizations is the mechanism, though this adds overhead' — would help engineers weigh whether the effort is justified.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page is well organized around the five resource-limit guardrail types present in the source: function memory, function timeout, container resource, resource type restriction, and resource count.
The opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the desired snippet length.
The distinction between preventive guardrails and reactive alarms is useful and matches the requested section instruction conceptually.
Tables make each guardrail property easy to scan, and the FAQ covers Stacktape-specific, AWS-adjacent, and comparison questions.
problems: Major source-grounding issue: the draft makes many Stacktape-specific behavior claims not present in `types/stacktape-config/guardrails.d.ts`, including when guardrails run, exact CLI commands evaluated, `GUARDRAIL` error behavior, failure timing, and the claim that no infrastructure changes are made.

Major source-grounding issue: Console and organization-scope claims are unsupported by the provided source. The source defines guardrail types and properties only; it does not say guardrails are configured in the Stacktape Console, organization-wide, apply to every project/stage, or cannot be overridden.

Major source-grounding issue: Lambda default memory `1024 MB`, default timeout `20 seconds`, explicit comparison behavior, and edge-function applicability are not grounded in the guardrails source. The source only says maximum memory and timeout are allowed for Lambda functions.

Major source-grounding issue: the container section invents evaluation mechanics. The source says `maxCpu` and `maxMemoryMB` limit container workloads; it does not say Stacktape checks `resources.cpu` / `resources.memory`, skips unset values, or applies the limit per workload rather than per container in multi-container workloads.

Major source-grounding issue: resource count semantics are unsupported. The source says `maxResources` is the maximum number of resources allowed per stack; it does not define how resources are counted, whether every named `resources` entry counts as one, or how this relates to generated CloudFormation resources.
Major source-grounding issue: violation examples and exact error messages are invented. The source provides no error format, message text, or remediation flow.

Several recommendations use concrete values such as `2048 MB`, `3008 MB`, `30 seconds`, `60 seconds`, `4 vCPU`, `8192 MB`, and `20-30 resources` without source support. Some are framed as advice, but the hard requirement says technical claims must be grounded in the provided files.

The FAQ repeats unsupported behavior claims, especially for delete behavior, default Lambda values, edge functions, skipped container checks, organization-level stage variation, and CloudFormation expansion.

The page is practical as a guide, but that practicality is achieved partly by inventing operational details absent from the source. For this assignment, source fidelity matters more than plausible guidance.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or qualify all CLI execution claims unless additional CLI/runtime source files are provided. From the current source, you can say only that guardrail types and properties are defined, not when or how commands evaluate them.

Remove or narrow all Stacktape Console and organization-scope claims unless console or guardrail runtime sources are provided. Do not state organization-wide behavior, no project override, or Console configuration flow from `guardrails.d.ts` alone.

Replace the Lambda default-memory/default-timeout discussion with source-grounded wording: `function-memory-limit` has optional `maxMemoryMB`; `function-timeout-limit` has optional `maxTimeoutSeconds`; both are described for Lambda functions. Do not claim default comparison values or edge-function coverage from this source.

Rewrite the container resource section to avoid unsupported evaluation details. Ground it only in `container-resource-limit.properties.maxCpu` and `maxMemoryMB`, described as maximum vCPU and memory in MB allowed for container workloads.

Rewrite the resource count section to avoid unsupported counting mechanics and CloudFormation-resource math. Ground it only in `resource-count-limit.properties.maxResources` as the maximum number of resources allowed per stack.
Delete the invented guardrail violation message examples, or replace them with cautious prose that the source does not specify the error format.

Audit every FAQ answer for unsupported Stacktape-specific behavior. Keep only facts directly present in `guardrails.d.ts` or clearly marked general AWS background that does not imply Stacktape behavior.

Remove unsupported concrete recommendation values or phrase them as examples only when the source itself gives examples: `1024`, `30`, `4`, `8192`, `20`, `open-search-domain`, and `redis-cluster` are grounded by JSDoc examples; other numeric recommendations are not.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact comparison table listing the five resource-limit guardrails, their `type` string, and their supported properties. That would be highly extractable and fully grounded in the source.
Make the page more explicit that resource-limit guardrails are one subset of the broader `GuardrailType` union, while linking or briefly naming the other categories only if those pages exist.
Shorten or remove cost-optimization anecdotes that depend on AWS pricing behavior unless pricing sources are included.

Consider replacing the long “Combining guardrails” example with a grounded matrix of supported resource-limit guardrails and a cautious note that other guardrail categories exist for deployment, security, and databases.

issues.statement: “The `function-memory-limit` guardrail caps the maximum memory … that any Lambda function or edge function in the stack can use… When a function does not explicitly set a `memory` value, Stacktape uses the default of 1024 MB for comparison.” Similar claims are made for `function-timeout-limit`, edge functions, and a 20-second default.
issues.reason: The source says these guardrails apply to “Lambda functions” and exposes `maxMemoryMB` / `maxTimeoutSeconds`. It does not mention edge functions or default memory/timeout values used during guardrail evaluation.
issues.suggestedFix: State only that `function-memory-limit` sets the maximum memory allowed for Lambda functions and `function-timeout-limit` sets the maximum timeout allowed for Lambda functions. Remove the edge-function and default-comparison claims unless source is added.

issues.statement: “The guardrail checks the `resources.cpu` and `resources.memory` values configured on each container workload. If a workload does not explicitly configure CPU or memory, no comparison is made and the guardrail does not block it — only explicitly set values are checked.”
issues.reason: The source defines `maxCpu` and `maxMemoryMB` for container workloads, but it does not specify the resource property paths inspected, whether defaults are considered, or whether omitted CPU/memory bypass validation.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the statement to the source-backed shape: `container-resource-limit` can set maximum vCPU and maximum memory allowed for container workloads. Avoid describing omitted-value behavior without validator/runtime source.

issues.statement: “The container resource limit checks per-workload values, not per-container values. In a multi-container workload, the limit applies to the total resources allocated to the task, not to each individual container within it.”
issues.reason: The source only says the limit is for “container workloads.” It does not define multi-container aggregation semantics or distinguish task-level from individual container-level validation.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the warning or rewrite cautiously: “The source defines this guardrail at the container-workload level; consult the workload resource docs for how CPU and memory are configured on each workload type.”

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the target length.
